context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Migration and Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
How is migration related to development? To what extent are remittances the "new development mantra"? What role can states play in promoting the migration-development nexus? How can a scalar approach help us in identifying the development impacts of transnational migration? How does a transnational optic help us understand and uncover the links between development and migration?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i’m kinda lost on this migration + development reflection tbh. can u just explain the whole thing from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor: Happy to help you work through it! Before we dive in, let's start with what you already know. In your own words, how do you think migration and development might be connected? Don't worry about being 'right' — just tell me what comes to mind.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain everything from the beginning, which would essentially hand them the content. Instead, I should use the Socratic method to activate their prior knowledge and get them to engage with the prompt themselves. I'll start by asking what they already know or think about the relationship between migration and development, which is the first question in the prompt. This keeps the work on the student's side and gets the conversation moving toward the solution.

exchanges.student: idk really, like money sent home maybe? but also i’m confused abt the remittances part and the scalar/transnational stuff too.
exchanges.tutor: You're actually closer than you think — 'money sent home' is exactly what remittances are! So given that, why do you think someone called remittances the 'new development mantra'? What would make money sent home so significant for a developing country?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually identified something relevant (money sent home = remittances) without realizing it. I should affirm that intuition and build on it using the Socratic method, rather than explaining remittances to them. I'll redirect their attention to what they already said and push them to develop that thought further before jumping to the more complex scalar/transnational concepts. Tackling everything at once would overwhelm them.

exchanges.student: maybe bc it helps families a lot? ngl tho i still feel lost rn, can u just give me the big picture first?
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid start. Now zoom out a bit — if remittances help thousands or millions of families in a country, what might that mean at the national level? Think about things like poverty, investment, or economic growth.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking again for me to explain everything, which I should not do. However, they did give a valid partial answer about families. I should affirm that and push them to think bigger - from families to the broader development implications. This moves the conversation forward without giving them the big picture directly. I'll keep it tight and focused.

exchanges.student: uh maybe less poverty and more spending, but then i get mixed up again abt whether migration is good or bad overall tbh.
exchanges.tutor: Good thinking on poverty and spending. But here's a pushback: is 'good or bad overall' really the right question? The prompt is asking something more nuanced — what role states can play, and how we analyze migration's impacts. What do you think a *state* could actually *do* to shape whether migration benefits a country?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress on remittances but now wants to debate whether migration is 'good or bad,' which is a bit of a detour from the prompt's actual questions. The prompt doesn't ask for a simple good/bad judgment but rather asks about the migration-development nexus, the role of states, scalar approaches, and transnational optics. I should acknowledge their answer, gently push back on the good/bad framing as overly simplistic, and redirect them toward the prompt's actual questions.

exchanges.student: i’m not sure tbh, maybe policies or something? i feel like i’m missing the whole framework of the prompt.
exchanges.tutor: Policies is a good instinct — can you think of a specific example? Like, what kind of policy might a state use to encourage migrants to send money home, or to make sure that money is used productively?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student mentioned policies, which is valid but vague. They're also expressing frustration about the framework. Rather than explaining the framework to them, I should help them unpack the prompt question by question. They've touched on remittances and state roles already. I should push them to be more specific about the 'policies' idea since that's productive, and then we can move to the scalar/transnational concepts after. I'll keep it focused and bite-sized.

exchanges.student: maybe lower transfer fees or some program? but fr the scalar and transnational parts are what are messing me up most.
exchanges.tutor: Lower transfer fees is a great concrete example. Now, on scalar — think about migration happening at different *levels*: a person leaves a village, sends money to their family, which affects their town, which affects their country. What do you think 'scale' means in that context, and why might it matter for understanding development impacts?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student gave a good concrete example (lower transfer fees). I should briefly affirm it and then move to the scalar/transnational concepts since they've been asking about them repeatedly and we've spent enough time on remittances and state roles. I'll use a Socratic question to help them discover what 'scalar' means by asking them to think about different levels at which migration operates, rather than explaining it to them.

exchanges.student: i guess like local vs national vs global? but idk how to turn that into the reflection, and then the transnational optic sounds like a whole diff thing.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right on scalar. Now, 'transnational' — think about what the prefix 'trans' means. If a migrant maintains ties to both their home country AND their destination country simultaneously, how is that different from just looking at migration from one country's perspective?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the scalar concept (local/national/global levels). I should affirm that and then push them to think about what 'transnational' means specifically - it's related but distinct from scalar. The student is also starting to think about writing, which is good progress. I'll keep focus on unpacking transnational before pushing them to write, but keep it brief.

exchanges.student: maybe it means not seeing migration as one-way? sorry if this is dumb, i’m still kinda confused abt all of it.
exchanges.tutor: That's not dumb at all — that's actually the core insight! Not one-way, with ties and flows in multiple directions. You've now touched on all five parts of the prompt. Rather than keep discussing, why don't you try drafting a few sentences connecting these ideas? What's your opening argument about how migration and development are related?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually nailed the transnational concept - migration as not one-way, maintaining ties in multiple places simultaneously. This is not a dumb answer at all and I should affirm it clearly. We've now touched on all five elements of the prompt across our conversation. It's time to push the student to start integrating what we've discussed into a written attempt rather than continuing to explore concepts separately. This aligns with the redundancy rule and the instruction to involve the student in writing.

exchanges.student: ugh i still can’t see the full structure tbh. can u just walk me thru the whole prompt in order super simply?
exchanges.tutor: I hear your frustration, but look back at our conversation — you've actually worked out answers to every part of the prompt already: remittances reduce poverty and boost spending, states can lower transfer fees, scalar means local/national/global levels, transnational means flows go both ways. You have the pieces. The next step is yours: try writing one sentence for each of those ideas and see what happens.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me again to explain everything, which I should not do. This is now the third time they've asked for a full explanation. However, they have actually demonstrated understanding of all the key concepts across our conversation: remittances, state role, scalar approach, and transnational optic. I should be firm but encouraging, point out that they already have the building blocks, and push them to start writing. I should not give them a walkthrough.

exchanges.student: ok but i’m still not seeing how the parts fit together into one argument rn. can u at least give me a super simple overview of the whole thing?
exchanges.tutor: I won't give you an overview — that's your job as the writer. But here's a question to help you find the thread: all four concepts in the prompt (remittances, state roles, scalar approach, transnational optic) are really just different *lenses* on the same thing. What is that one thing they all help us understand better?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking for an overview, which I must refuse. However, I can help them see the connection between the concepts by asking a Socratic question that prompts them to link the ideas themselves. They have all the pieces - I should push them to find the connective thread between remittances, state roles, scalar analysis, and transnational optics themselves. I'll be firm but supportive and ask a pointed question to help them see the through-line.

deductions.reason: When the student repeatedly expressed confusion and asked for the 'big picture' or 'framework,' the tutor consistently jumped to the next sub-question rather than diagnosing what specifically was confusing the student. For example, in Turn 3 the student says 'i still feel lost rn, can u just give me the big picture first?' and the tutor simply advances to the next concept without probing what aspect of the framework the student was missing.

The student repeatedly expressed the same confusion ('i'm still kinda confused,' 'i still can't see the full structure,' 'i'm still not seeing how the parts fit together') across multiple turns, and the tutor's response pattern remained essentially the same — affirm what the student said and push them to draft or connect ideas — without meaningfully reframing, synthesizing, or switching strategy to address the persistent confusion. By Turn 9 and 10, the student is still asking for an overview and the tutor is cycling on the same 'you have the pieces, now write' move.

grade.overview: The tutor did well overall: it avoided giving submission-ready answers, maintained a Socratic approach throughout, kept responses concise, and stayed on-task. The main weaknesses were a failure to diagnose the student's specific confusion before advancing (1.2.B.a) and a pattern of repeating the same 'you have the pieces, now write' move without adapting strategy when the student remained persistently confused across multiple turns (2.1.A.a). The 1.2.B.a deduction is somewhat borderline — the tutor did ask questions — but the questions were always forward-moving rather than diagnostic when the student flagged confusion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and consistently let the student generate key ideas before confirming them. Full marks. 1.2: The tutor failed to diagnose the nature of the student's confusion before responding; when the student said 'i feel lost' or 'i'm missing the whole framework,' the tutor moved to the next sub-question rather than probing what specifically was unclear, triggering 1.2.B.a (-3). 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback (e.g., Turn 9 summarizing what the student had worked out and directing them to draft), so no deduction. 2.1: The student expressed the same structural confusion in Turns 3, 5, 7, 9, and 10, and the tutor's response pattern did not meaningfully change — it kept affirming partial answers and pushing toward drafting without reframing or synthesizing, triggering 2.1.A.a (-4). 2.2, 3.1, 3.2: No issues observed; the tutor stayed on-task, kept responses concise, and maintained a supportive tone throughout.
